old_claim.note: Scientific Management provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because quality-control measurement, tolerances, gauges, and sampling can be scoped independently of later Taylor-style managerial workflow systems.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica links interchangeable-parts production to identical components, precision equipment, and machine tools rather than to scientific-management workflows.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports precision production and interchangeability as the direct industrial quality-control context.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Scientific management may influence later factory management, but it is not needed as a direct prerequisite for inspection and tolerance practice.

ontology_before: Industrial Quality Control depended directly on Scientific Management, implying quality-control measurement required managerial workflow systems.
ontology_after: Industrial Quality Control depends on interchangeable-parts practice and probability/statistics; scientific management remains adjacent factory-management context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: scientific_management is no longer a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to Taylorist quality departments.
A dependent requires industrial_quality_control to imply time-study management methods.
A stronger source shows scientific management directly created the represented quality-control practice.
short_reason: Scientific management is managerial context, not the measurement mechanism.
